coredata.dc:title: N-substituted CQDs impregnated by Fe3O4 heterostructure: Bifunctional catalyst for electro-catalytic and photo-catalytic detection of an environmental hazardous organic pollutant
coredata.dc:creator: Ragu, Sasikumar
Kim, Byungki
Chen, Shen-Ming
Ishfaque, Asif
Kang, Kwang-Mo
coredata.dc:description: ortho-Nitroaniline (o-NA) compounds are deemed to be a strongly toxic pollutant in nature and potentially carcinogenic; however, they are frequently utilized to synthesize dyes, pesticides, medicines, fungicides, pigments, and other organic chemicals. Their detection in an aqueous medium is fundamentally required to avoid the potential hazardous being created by these compounds. In this study, a novel sensor based on an Iron oxide (Fe3O4) containing highly dispersed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs@Fe3O4 NFs) was demonstrated for the electrochemical detection of o-NA using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. N-CQDs@Fe3O4 NFs were synthesized by hydrothermal method and studied by various analytical and spectroscopy techniques, which collectively reveal that the as-prepared composite has superior physical and chemical properties. The DPV study indicated that the o-NA sensor had a good limit of detection, linear range, and sensitivity in the range of 1.2 nm, 0.03–386.84 μM, and 36.5575 μA μM−1 cm−2, respectively, along with the sensor showed superior sensitivity when compared to the previously reported modified electrodes. Further, N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs worked as heterogeneous catalysts for the photocatalytic reduction of o-NA to o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) in an aqueous medium. The reaction was examined under UV–Visible spectroscopy, and the complete photocatalytic reduction was observed for the N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs in about 6 min with 96% as compared to other control samples; thus, authenticating the superiority of the synthesized composite in rendering the real-time applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ortho-Nitroaniline (o-NA) compounds are deemed to be a strongly toxic pollutant in nature and potentially carcinogenic; however, they are frequently utilized to synthesize dyes, pesticides, medicines, fungicides, pigments, and other organic chemicals. Their detection in an aqueous medium is fundamentally required to avoid the potential hazardous being created by these compounds. In this study, a novel sensor based on an Iron oxide (Fe3O4) containing highly dispersed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs@Fe3O4 NFs) was demonstrated for the electrochemical detection of o-NA using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. N-CQDs@Fe3O4 NFs were synthesized by hydrothermal method and studied by various analytical and spectroscopy techniques, which collectively reveal that the as-prepared composite has superior physical and chemical properties. The DPV study indicated that the o-NA sensor had a good limit of detection, linear range, and sensitivity in the range of 1.2 nm, 0.03–386.84 μM, and 36.5575 μA μM−1 cm−2, respectively, along with the sensor showed superior sensitivity when compared to the previously reported modified electrodes. Further, N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs worked as heterogeneous catalysts for the photocatalytic reduction of o-NA to o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) in an aqueous medium. The reaction was examined under UV–Visible spectroscopy, and the complete photocatalytic reduction was observed for the N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs in about 6 min with 96% as compared to other control samples; thus, authenticating the superiority of the synthesized composite in rendering the real-time applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: ortho-Nitroaniline (o-NA) compounds are toxic in nature and their acute or chronic contact can lead to unwanted side effects on the body. In general, their production is linked with anthropogenic activities, like pharmaceutical products, explosives, and the synthesis of dyes. Further, their traces are also found in military plants and textile industries' waste. Their presence in water is harmful to aquatic life and produce symptoms of dizziness, nausea, and headache in human. Therefore, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) has declared o-NA as a harmful waste and toxic pollutant (EPA, Washington., 1994). The literature is abundant with several traditional physicochemical and biological treatment methods; but, due to the extremely stable and bio-refractory characteristics, the o-NA compound's removal with these methods is a tedious process. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an easy, portable, and cost-effective method for rapid detection and efficient removal of o-NA compounds, particularly in wastewater. Compared with other methods, the electrochemical and photocatalytic methods can serve this purpose efficiently. The chemical structure and the properties of o-NA are listed in Table S1.

In literature, different analytical methods are reported for the detection of the o-NA in real samples, like liquid-phase microextraction (LPE) and HPLC (Yazdi and Yazdi et al., 2009), solid-phase extraction (SPE) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with ultraviolet detection (UVD) (Tong et al., 2010), gas chromatography (GC) (Kataoka et al., 1996; Chiang et al., 2008), ultraviolet spectroscopy (UV) (Niazi et al., 2007; Ghasemi et al., 2005; Hasani et al., 2008), and capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Guo et al., 2006). However, these methods carry some drawbacks, like time-consuming, tedious, complicated post-treatment, and expensive instruments. Therefore, developing simple probably portable techniques having excellent efficiency even for a minute amount of o-NA has garnered significant attention. In this aspect, the electrochemical techniques are well-practiced in recent times owing to their convenience in usage, cost-effectiveness, fast, portable instruments, and good sensitivity and selectivity (Palakollu et al., 2017; Karthik et al., 2017), and are also considered in the present study. For instance, Arbab et al. (Arbab et al., 2012) reported a sensitive DTD/AgNPs/CPE electrode device for o-NA with a limit of detection (LOD) of 230 nM. Bai et al. (2015) reported AgNPs-POSS/rGO modified GCE exhibiting LOD of 360 nM. Recently, Dong et al. (2020) fabricated a highly sensitive and selective nanocomposite with β-CD-GQDs/GCE for the electrochemical detection of o-NA. They reported a wide linear range (WLR) of 0.06–200 μM and LOD of 6.6 nM.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) constitute a new type of carbon nanoparticles, and have unique green, low-toxicity, eco-friendliness, and abundant physical and chemical properties compared to conventional metal-based quantum dots (Wang et al., 2015). Among them, N-CQDs have gathered significant attention owing to their exclusive properties, like fluorescence emission, photo-stability, and good conductivity, which make them a suitable candidate for many applications, particularly in optical detection, bio-sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, light-emitting diodes, photo-catalysis, and fluorescent ink (Gholinejad et al., 2016; Lim et al., 2015). Their preparation can be accomplished by different methods, like laser ablation, hydrothermal synthesis, microwave synthesis, and electrochemical etching (Baker et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2010; Li et al., 2012; Neubeck et al., 2010). Further, N-CQDs have outstanding support for catalyst stabilization when associated with other carbon allotropes, such as graphene and carbon nanotube.

Magnetic metal oxides have excellent physical-chemical properties, and have profound applications in various fields, like energy storage, catalysis, electrochemical sensors, hyperthermia-based therapy, and drug delivery. Among them, Iron oxide (Fe3O4) is a superparamagnetic and good oxidation material owing to its highly active nature and very easy to react all carbon and carbon-based materials. Additionally, magnetic Fe3O4 has no functional groups for further modifications. Therefore, it is predominantly desirable to modify the magnetic metal oxides’ external phase for industrial applications (Sasikumar et al., 2017). Recently, there are some reports available on the electro-catalysis and photo-catalysis performance of N-CQDs and Fe3O4 where they are used as a catalyst (Ma et al., 2022; Saipanya et al., 2022; Madima et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2022). It is well-known that the surface morphology and crystalline nature of the material play a significant role in electro-catalytic and photo-catalytic properties due to fast electron transportation, higher surface area, low accumulation nature, and interconnection with channels. In this aspect, we designed and developed a flower shape N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs with good crystallinity (55%) via the eco-friendly method.

In this work, we developed Iron oxide encapsulated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots nano-flowers (N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs) for the efficient electro-catalytic detection and photoreduction of environmentally hazardous organic pollutant o-NA. The incorporation of Fe3O4 and N-CQD have synergistically superior active sites and fast electron transportation properties under pH-regulated electrolyte condition for the detection of o-NA. The N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst binds with o-NA via hydrogen bonding and π – π interactions. The portable, cost-effective, and sensitive voltammetric method has been studied. The achieved LOD, LR, and sensitivity for o-NA detection were ca. 1.2 nM, ca. 0.03–386.84 μM, and ca. 36.5575 μA μM−1 cm−2, respectively. For real-time samples, N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst exhibited excellent electrochemical sensing of o-NA in mineral water and tap water, which authenticates the potential of N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst for practical applications. Further, the as-prepared nanocatalyst also demonstrated an excellent photocatalytic reduction of o-NA under visible light illumination. Scheme 1 is demonstrating synthesis and bifunctional applications of N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs.

The as-prepared N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs were characterized using PXRD, FT-IR, Raman spectra, BET, FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, EIS, CV, LSV, and DPV methods. The synthesis and characterization details are available in the Supplementary information.

Fig. 1 a shows the characteristic PXRD patterns of the as-prepared N-CQD and N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs. For N-CQD, the broad peak appears between 15 and 30° due to its amorphous nature. Similarly, for N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs, diffraction peaks detected at 2θ ca. 21.31°, 30.37°, 35.22°, 37.48°, 45.41°, 50.74°, 55.59°, and 60.28° are corresponding to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes, respectively. The obtained diffraction peaks are closely matched and conformed with Fe3O4 NFs. Moreover, the broad peak between 15 and 30° indicates the typical amorphous nature of Fe3O4 NFs. According to the JCPDS card #19-0629, the diffraction peaks are cubic spinel structured and are in good arrangement with the previously reported articles (Hu et al., 2017). The instrumental development (βhkl) for every diffraction peak of Fe3O4 is adjusted by using the following relation (Eqn (1)):

The average nanocrystallite size is calculated with Debye-Scherrer's equation eqn (2) (Hu et al., 2014):

where D = crystallite size, K = shape factor (0.89), and λ = wavelength of Cu kα radiation. From the calculations, the average crystallite size of the Fe3O4 in N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs is about 31 nm. The crystalline structure and crystallographic information of Fe3O4 were visualized using VESTA software and shown in Fig. 1b, c. The PXRD analysis confirms that the as-synthesized Fe3O4 belongs to the cubic P1 space group. The Fe3O4 crystal has the unit cell parameters a = b = c = 8.53335 Å with α = β = γ = 90° and the cell density 4.95 g cm−3. Other analytical parameters, such as peak position (2θ), FWHM (2θ), d-spacing (Å), crystalline size - D (nm), intensity (%), lattice strain (ε), and dislocation density (δ) are calculated and tabulated in Table S2.

FT-IR spectrum was carried out to study the functional groups of N-CQD and N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs as depicted in Fig. 1d. For N-CQD, two characteristic peaks appear at 1630.36 and 3314.71 cm−1 due to the –OH bending and stretching vibrations (Chadha et al., 2022). For N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs, sharp two peaks are observed at 473.19 and 556.30 cm−1 corresponding to Fe–O band (M − O band) in Fe3O4, while the –OH stretching vibrations are detected at 3249.08 cm−1 (Xu et al., 2022). Further, two characteristic peaks appear at 1082.82 and 1224.42 cm−1 conforming the –C-O (alkoxy) and –C-O (epoxy) stretching vibrations due to the polyaromatic hydrocarbons and epoxide/ether functional groups. Some other new bonds appear at 708.26 and 910.60 cm−1 and can be attributed to Fe–O bond formation between the carboxyl groups in N-CQDs and the iron atoms in the Fe3O4 NPs (Zhou et al., 2022). The –N-H bending vibration peak appears at 1629.36 cm−1.

Raman spectroscopy is a significant analytical technique to study the structure and advantages of the as-prepared NF, and the Raman spectra of N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs were noted from the 900-1800 cm−1 range, as shown in Fig. 1e. The D and G bands of CQDs are present at 1328 and 1576 cm−1, respectively. The D/G intensity ratio is increased due to the existence of Fe3O4 NFs on the surface of CQDs. Fe3O4 NFs are incorporated on the N-CQDs surface by a strong covalent interaction. Charges are moved from the Fe3O4 (CB) to the CQDs (C 2p-derived states) (Sasikumar et al., 2017). Furthermore, the surface area of the N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs was studied by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm, as shown in Fig. 1f. The N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs show an isotherm curve close to typical type III isotherm (IUPAC classification) with a hysteresis loop from P/P0 = 0.9 to 0.4 suggesting the existence of micro/mesoporous particles. Consequently, the specific surface area of N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs is ca. 43.03 m2 g−1 by using the BET equation (Kanchana et al., 2015). The comparison of the current study results with the previously reported similar catalyst results is tabulated in Table S3. These results and the comparison table collectively suggest that the as-synthesized catalyst N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs tends to possess excellent active sites; thereby, it shows excellent performance in terms of fast electron transportation and surface area.

FE-SEM, EDX, and TEM are the most commonly used analysis method to study the chemical composition and morphology of the as-prepared N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs. FE-SEM images in Fig. 1g-i show different magnifications of the as-prepared N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs consisting of randomly dispersed NFs with uniform spherical, non-aggregation and the spheres are monodispersed with average diameter size ranging from 100 to 500 nm. From Fig. 1j, the EDX spectrum reveals that the chemical composition of the as-prepared N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs is comprised of C, O, N, and Fe without the presence of any impurities. The weight percentages of the C, O, N, and Fe are 25.36, 17.21, 22.89, and 34.54, respectively. The atomic and weight percentage of the elements presented in as-prepared N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs are tabulated and displayed in Fig. 1j (Inset). TEM images in Fig. 1k-m, reveal that the as-synthesized Fe3O4 shells have flower shapes, and are composed of numerous N-CQDs.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to study the mechanism of the charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface with different modified electrodes, such as bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, and N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE in 0.1 M KCl/5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution. The fitted Nyquist plots obtained for different modified electrodes are shown in Figs. S1(a and b), where the Randles equivalent circuit model was employed in the fitting of the impedance data as shown in Fig. S1b (Inset). Here, R ct is the interfacial electron-transfer resistance, Zw is the Warburg diffusion process, C dl is the double-layer resistance, and R s is the electrolyte solution resistance. The R ct values of bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, and N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE are 282.21, 295.25, 192.11, and 168.39 Ω, respectively. The R ct value of N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE (168.39 Ω) is significantly lower than the other modified electrodes. A similar trend is also found in the diameter of the semicircle (Fig. S1b), where it is extremely low for N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE when compared with other modified electrodes. These results suggest that Fe3O4 and N-CQD enhance the conductivity of the modified electrode by fast e− transfer from the electrolyte medium to the modified electrode interface. This study highlights the excellent e−transfer performance of the suggested modified electrode, and for comparison, the electrode parameters of the other reported modified electrodes are listed in Table 1 and the calculated electrochemical redox peak anodic and cathodic current of the different modified electrodes are tabulated in Table S4.

Further, from Fig. S1c, the electro-catalytic active surface area (ESCA) is calculated by using Randles-Sevcik equ 3 (Dinesh et al., 2017),

where D, n, ʋ, A, C, and Ip represent the diffusion coefficient (7.60 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), number of electrons involved in the electro-catalytic reaction, sweeping rate (mV s−1), active surface area (cm2), concentration (mol cm−3), and redox peak current (μA), respectively. The electro-catalytic surface area of the bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, and N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE is calculated to be 0.038, 0.047, 0.061, and 0.091 cm2, respectively. This data reveals that the electro-catalytic active surface area of the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE is higher than the other modified electrodes; therefore, N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE is a suitable material that can be used to improve the electrode sensitivity due to its fast electron transportation kinetics between the redox couples, which is enabled by the improved surface area and reduced particle size.

Further, electro-catalytic kinetics was studied using redox peak current (μA) for the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE using CV curves of the KCl/[Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution at various sweeping measurements, as shown in Fig. S1d. The redox peak current is gradually increasing while increasing the sweeping rate ranges from 20 to 300 mV s−1, which reveals that electronic transportation enhances with increasing the sweep rates. A linear correlation was observed when the redox peak current (μA) was plotted (Fig. S1e) between the square root of the scan rates (ʋ1/2) vs current (μA) indicating a linear regression equation of I p, a (μA) = 0.4111 [o-NA] (μM) + 117.66 with a regression coefficient of R2 = 0.9943 and I p, c (μA) = −0.4176 [o-NA] (μM) – 91.832 with a regression coefficient of R2 = 0.9964.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of nitro groups at different modified electrodes and influence of electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: The possible electro-reduction mechanism of o-NA at N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE is shown in Fig. 2 a, which represents four electrons (e−) and two protons (H+) involved on the surface. Fig. 2b shows the CV response of bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, and N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE, which was examined in the absence of 200 μM of o-NA in 0.05 M PBS (pH 7.0) at the sweep rate of 50 mV s−1. In the absence of o-NA, there is no visible peak appeared for bare GCE and other modified electrodes. Further, CVs response of bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, and N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE was recorded in the presence of 200 μM of o-NA under the same experimental conditions, as shown in Fig. 2c. The observed reduction peak current (μA) is bare GCE (I p, c = 4.796 μA), N-CQD/GCE (I p, c = 2.912 μA), Fe3O4/GCE (I p, c = 3.560 μA), and N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE (I p, c = 11.852 μA) and the current response of the bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, and N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE also shown in the bar diagram form in Fig. 2d. The highest reduction peak current is noticed for N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE due to its more active sites, higher electro-catalytic surface area, and faster electron transportation; whereas, other modified electrodes show lower reduction peak current due to the sluggish electron transportation and very few active sites. These obtained results are also consistent with EIS analysis (Figs. S1a and b) (Karthik et al., 2016; Sasikumar et al., 2017).

The influence of pH was examined by using PBS with different pH ranging from 3.0 to 11.0 under N2 saturated conditions with a sweeping rate of 50 mV s−1 as shown in Fig. 2e. From the figure, the reduction peak current increases gradually from 3.0 to 7.0 pH, and the maximum current is observed at pH 7.0; after that, it decreases till 11.0 with unsatisfactory reduction peaks. When the pH increases, the peak potential also moves towards the negative potential representing that the nitro group reduction process is assisted by the availability of protons (H+). These results indicate that pH 7.0 is an optimum electro-catalytic reduction of o-NA for all the experiments. The corresponding plot with a different pH vs cathodic peak potential (V) and peak current (μA) is also given in Fig. 2f. The π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding of o-NA with N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE are shown in Scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of additions, sweeping rates, and determination of o-NA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a shows the CV curves obtained at N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE in PBS (pH 7.0) containing various concentrations (100-500 μM) of o-NA at a sweeping rate of 50 mV s−1. From the figure, there is no visible reduction peak when N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE is recorded in the absence of o-NA. However, as we have introduced 100 μM of o-NA in the buffer solution, the peak starts to appear and linearly increases with increasing o-NA concentration from 100 to 500 μM. Fig. 3d shows the plot of the cathodic peak current versus o-NA concentrations with linear regression calculation as I pc (μA) = 22.639 [o-NA][μM] + 6.6456 and regression coefficient R2 = 0.9921. The influence of different sweep rates ranging from 20 to 200 mV s−1 for o-NA with 200 μM of o-NA was investigated using CV in pH 7.0 (0.05 M PBS) under saturated N2 conditions. In Fig. 3b, the o-NA reduction peak current (I pc) increases with increasing sweep rates, which indicates that the rate of electron transportation is equal to the rate of mass transportation. Moreover, when the reduction currents (I pc) increase, the sweep rates also increase. Fig. 3e and Fig. S2a show the plot between reduction currents (I pc) and the square root of the sweep rates and the plot between reduction currents (I pc) and the sweep rates, respectively, with linear regression equation as I pc (μA) = 0.7396V1/2 (mV s−1) + 0.6329 and the coefficient value R2 = 0.9911. In Fig. 3e, the R2 value is very close to 1, which labels the diffusion-controlled process. To further confirm the process, a plot between the logarithm of cathodic peak current and the logarithm of sweeping rate for o-NA is shown in Fig. S2b. The slope value for the reduction peak response for o-NA is given 0.47, which is nearly equal to the theoretical value of 0.5 (diffusion-controlled). This concludes that the reduction mechanism of o-NA at the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE is a diffusion-controlled process (Lin et al., 2012).

The electro-catalytic reduction of o-NA was done by N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE using DPV, which is a highly sensitive technique for the electro-catalytic determination of o-NA as compared to CV. In Fig. 3c, DPV depicts the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE response with different increasing concentrations in pH 7.0 (0.05 M PBS) under N2 saturated conditions. A well-sharp cathodic peak is found at −0.786 V which increases while increasing the various concentrations of o-NA, and affords a wide linear range (WLR) from 0.03 to 386.84 μM. In Fig. 3f, a plot between o-NA concentrations versus reduction peak current (I pc) is shown with a linear regression equation I pc (V) = 2.6358 [o-NA](μM) + 1.1466 and R2 = 0.9959. Figs. S3(a and b) display a DPV response of a lower concentration (0.03–13.34 μM) and a plot between o-NA lower concentrations versus reduction peak current (I pc) with a linear regression equation I pc (V) = 0.0414 [o-NA](μM) + 1.0457 and R2 = 0.9912. The sensitivity and limit of detection (LOD) values are calculated to be 36.5575 (±0.002) μA μM−1 cm−2 and 1.2 nm, respectively, according to the following IUPAC formulas 4 and 5,

LOD values were compared with various electrodes having different substrates, such as CPE and GCE. The considered electrodes include DTD/AgNPs/CPE (Arbab et al., 2012), AgNPs-POSS/rGO/GCE (Bai et al., 2015), β-CD-GQDs/GCE (Dong et al., 2020), DHCBAQS/graphene-nafion/GCE (Lin et al., 2012), Ag (Laghrib et al., 2017), GCE (Pfeifer et al., 2016), CoSnO/GCE (Palpandi et al., 2020), and U-BMO/f-CNF/GCE (Krishnapandi et al., 2021) as shown in Table 1. In the noteworthy that all the previously reported modified electrodes like DTD/AgNPs/CPE (Arbab et al., 2012), AgNPs-POSS/rGO/GCE (Bai et al., 2015), β-CD-GQDs/GCE (Dong et al., 2020), DHCBAQS/graphene-nafion/GCE (Lin et al., 2012), Ag (Laghrib et al., 2017), GCE (Pfeifer et al., 2016), CoSnO/GCE (Palpandi et al., 2020), and U-BMO/f-CNF/GCE (Krishnapandi et al., 2021) display higher LOD values when compared with N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE. Remarkably, the proposed sensor's electro-catalytic reaction is outstanding due to the following key factors: (i) simple material construction and components, (ii) the incorporation of Fe3O4 and N-CQD enhances synergistically superior active sites and fast electron transportation properties, and (iii) it has distinctive electronic and electro-catalytic properties that have drawn significant interest for researchers and real-time industrial applications (Dong et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2015; Gholinejad et al., 2016). Considering these remarkable properties, N-CQD/Fe3O4 is an outstanding electrode for the electro-catalytic detection of o-NA. The comparison of detection limitations such as LR and LOD of o-NA with other reports is mentioned in Table 1 (Arbab et al., 2012; Bai et al., 2015; Dong et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2012; Laghrib et al., 2017; Pfeifer et al., 2016; Palpandi et al., 2020; Krishnapandi et al., 2021). Further, the electro-catalytic studies of N-CQD/Fe3O4 has been compared with a similar type of modifiers in Table S5, and the data authenticates the superior electro-catalytic performance of N-CQD/Fe3O4. Therefore, our modified N-CQD/Fe3O4 has significant potential to be an efficient candidate for the electro-catalytic detection of o-NA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, accumulation time, repeatability, operational, and storage stability
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE electrode was carried out using DPV for o-NA in the presence of different interfering compounds, like 4-nitrophenol (4NP), 2-aminophenol (2-AP), 3-aminophenol (3-AP), acetaminophen (ACP), flutamide (FLU), carbendazim (CBZ), chlorine (Cl−), potassium (K+), sodium (Na+), hydroquinone (HQ), and dopamine (DA) in PBS (pH 7.0). The reduced peak current intensity of o-NA Relative error (%) of different interferents for o-NA is 4NP (1.25%), 2-AP (2.15%), 3-AP (1.51%), ACP (2.64%), FLU (3.31%), CBZ (2.10), Cl− (2.67%), K+ (3.69%), Na+ (1.25%), HQ (1.66%), and DA (3.82%), respectively (Fig. S4a) (Ragu et al., 2016; Sasikumar et al., 2018a,b; Sasikumar and Kim, 2022; Yazdi et al., 2009a). Different interfering compounds with o-NA electro-catalytic detection relative error (%) were calculated, and the outcomes are shown in Fig. S4a. As expected, N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE displays a good current response only for o-NA as compared to other interferences. When the other interferential biomolecules are added, still the current response is insignificant. The obtained results suggest that the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE has displayed excellent selectivity and preference for o-NA and makes it suitable for other practical applications.

Accumulation time is a significant parameter for the electro-catalytic performance of the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE sensor. The effect of the accumulation time of the o-NA was studied from 0 to 300 s with 200 μM of o-NA at 0.05 M PBS (pH 7.0) as depicted in Fig. S4b. The high cathodic peak is observed at 120 s for o-NA. Therefore, the 120 s for o-NA are the optimal accumulation time for the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE sensor.

In Fig. S4c, N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE was investigated for repeatability study with five measurements in 0.05 M PBS (pH 7.0) under N2 saturated conditions containing 200 μM of o-NA. The RSD value calculated for o-NA is about 0.31%. These values are fairly acceptable for practical scenarios for sensing o-NA. Furthermore, the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE operational (50 consecutive cycles) and storage stability (30 days) were performed in 0.05 M PBS (pH 7.0) under N2 saturated conditions containing 200 μM of o-NA in Figs. S4d and e. The RSD values for operational and storage stability are about ∼0.85 and ∼0.33% for o-NA, respectively. These findings reveal that the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE sensor has good stability towards the electro-catalytic sensing of o-NA.

The practical applicability of N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE NFs was investigated for the electro-catalytic reduction of o-NA in mineral and tap water that were probably contaminated with trace levels of o-NA. 50 mL of tap water sample was collected from the National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan. 50 mL of mineral water sample was bought from a local convenience store located in Taiwan. These samples were transferred and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The lower portion was discarded and the supernatant portion was collected for real sample analysis. Samples were filtered using Whatman No. 1 filter paper, and the presence of o-NA was tested by using N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE. The presence of o-NA was not detected in the water samples. To check the validity of the result, known concentrations of o-NA (0.013 g, i.e., 0.01 M concentration) were added into the 1.0 mL of real samples and the spiked concentration of o-NA was determined by the standard addition method (Fig. 4 a–d). These observations clearly indicate that the N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE finds potential application in o-NA detection in water samples, and the DPV results are also tabulated in Table S6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of o-NA over N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the catalytic properties of N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst that had been selected for the model reduction reaction of o-NA into o-PD. The reaction was easily followed because the formation product for the reduction reaction was only the development of the reaction can be monitored by UV–Vis absorbance (Le et al., 2014; Chang et al., 2009; Subhra et al., 2006; Chiou et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. S5, the mixture of yellow-colored o-NA and NaBH4 solution exhibits two typical absorbance peaks at 280 and 412 nm. Nevertheless, the mixed solution little decreases the intensity of two peaks after 150 min. Furthermore, when the pure Fe3O4 or N-CQDs were added to the mixed solution, the intensity of the absorbance peak was changed insignificantly even after above 30 and 36 min, respectively (Fig. 5 a and b). This reduction reaction indicates that it can't continue without a nanocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 5c, when the N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst is added to the mixed solution of o-NA and NaBH4, the intensity of the two absorbance peaks decreases gradually as o-NA was reduced to o-PD. It is observed that the N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst exhibits greater catalytic activity and completed the reaction time in only 6 min, while some extra time is necessary for the other catalytic samples. The linear relationship between ln (Ct/C0) is shown in Fig. 5d, where C0 is the initial concentration of o-NA and Ct is the concentration of o-NA at reaction time t, which confirms the first-order kinetics reaction. Degradation efficiency (%) for N-CQD, Fe3O4, and N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst is about 13, 19, and 96%, respectively. Possible reaction mechanism for the reduction of o-NA by NaBH4 over the N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst into o-PD is shown in Fig. 6 . One step further, a comparison was made between the current study on the N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst and the previously reported research work on the photocatalysis of o-PD under UV, and the results are summarized in Table 2 .

Recyclability is fundamentally required for the catalyst in terms of economic and practical aspects. Therefore, the reusability of N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst was studied in o-NA reduction. N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst was recovered using an external magnet after each photo-catalytic experiment, rinsed with water, and dried at RT. It showed that the N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst was successfully catalyzed and reduction of o-NA for five consecutive times. The FE-SEM (Fig. 7 a) and TEM images (Fig. 7b) of N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst after five cycles reveal that its crystalline size and surface morphology are almost the same as that of the fresh catalyst (Fig. 1g-i and (k-m)). Fig. 7c shows that the PXRD patterns of fresh and recycled N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst, which do not indicate any remarkable changes in PXRD patterns in terms of crystallinity, representing the stability of N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst. The characteristic peaks of N-CQD and Fe3O4 remain the same; however, the peak intensity decreases. It is confirming the excellent chemical stability and recyclability of the N-CQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst.

In summary, the preparation of N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs was carried out by following the hydrothermal method and carefully studied by different analytical, electro and photo-catalytic methods. We effectively demonstrated the modified electrode (N-CQD/Fe3O4/GCE) for the electrochemical sensing of o-NA, and these NFs were also utilized for photocatalytic reduction of o-NA for the first time. The electro-catalytic detection towards o-NA using CV and DPV techniques showed that the as-prepared catalyst provided a number of active sites, along with the synergetic effect between the N-CQD and Fe3O4 brought excellent electron transportation and higher conductivity. Furthermore, the modified electrode has a high surface area, reproducible, reusable, and good stability. The practical implementation with mineral water and tap water showed excellent recovery results which make it a promising electrode for electro-catalytic detecting o-NA in complicated environmental samples, In the photocatalytic reduction of o-NA, NaBH4 was employed as a donor under visible light and it was compared with N-CQD/Fe3O4 NFs photocatalyst. Interestingly, the decrease in photocatalytic effectiveness was 96% in about 6 min as compared to other control samples. This study endorsed the potential of the proposed nanocomposite for real samples and perspective practical industrial applications.
